data.title: MLPs Vs. Midstream Corporates: How AMZA Seeks To Capture Income Opportunities
data.description:
Midstream energy companies operate much like toll roads, collecting fees for transporting oil and natural gas rather than relying on commodity prices. That fee-based model has long appealed to income investors, but corporate structure plays a decisive role in how much cash ultimately reaches shareholders. Midstream firms generally operate as traditional C-corporations or as master limited partnerships (MLPs), and the distinction can materially affect income received. Many pipeline corporations offer dividends in the mid-single digits, while established MLPs have historically delivered meaningfully higher distributions.¹

The difference stems largely from taxation. MLPs typically avoid corporate income tax, allowing more operating cash flow to be passed directly to investors.¹ Corporations, by contrast, are taxed at the entity level before distributing dividends. The trade-off is complexity. Investors in MLPs are treated as partners and receive Schedule K-1 tax forms, which can complicate filings and discourage participation.¹ These frictions have kept some income-focused investors on the sidelines despite the sector’s attractive yields.

Structural Change and the Natural Gas Shift

The midstream sector has evolved meaningfully over the past decade. Once characterized by complex partnership structures and incentive distribution rights, many operators have simplified governance, reduced leverage, and aligned payouts with sustainable cash flow. Some firms have even converted from MLPs to corporate structures to broaden their investor base.¹

What has not changed is the underlying business model. Pipelines earn fees based on volumes transported under long-term contracts, insulating cash flows from short-term commodity price volatility. That stability has become more pronounced as the industry pivots toward natural gas. Roughly three-quarters of North American midstream infrastructure now centers on natural gas rather than oil, reflecting demand from power generation, residential heating, and liquefied natural gas (LNG) exports.²

Natural gas demand tends to be less cyclical than oil consumption, and the expansion of U.S. LNG exports has created an additional long-term demand channel.² At the same time, management teams have become more disciplined, funding capital projects internally and prioritizing balance sheet strength. Despite these improvements, many midstream companies continue to trade below historical valuation averages, suggesting that the market has yet to fully recognize the sector’s durability.²

How AMZA Packages Midstream Income

The InfraCap MLP ETF (AMZA, the Fund) aims to bridge the gap between high-yield MLP income and investor convenience. Rather than owning individual partnerships directly, investors gain diversified exposure through a single ETF and receive a standard Form 1099-DIV instead of multiple K-1s.³

AMZA is actively managed, allowing portfolio managers to adjust holdings based on fundamentals and market conditions rather than tracking a static index. The Fund typically holds a diversified mix of major MLPs and related midstream companies. To help enhance income, AMZA employs moderate leverage and selectively writes covered call options, generating additional cash flow while supporting monthly distributions.⁴ These tools can increase income potential but may also amplify volatility during periods of market stress.

For investors comfortable with those trade-offs, AMZA offers a streamlined way to access the steady cash flows of U.S. energy infrastructure. It transforms a structurally complex segment of the market into a single, tradeable vehicle while preserving the core attraction of midstream investing: potentially durable income backed by essential assets.

Footnotes

  1. Infrastructure Capital Advisors. Incorporated Pipeline or Master Limited Partnership (MLP)? Pros and Cons. Infrastructure Capital Substack. October 2025.

  2. Infrastructure Capital Advisors. Midstream’s Pivot to Natural Gas: Why Gas-Focused MLPs Could Outperform. Infrastructure Capital Substack. November 24, 2025.

  3. Nasdaq. Skip the K-1 Paperwork: The InfraCap MLP ETF Seeks Convenient Income for Energy Investors. Press release. October 13, 2025.

  4. Virtus Investment Partners. InfraCap MLP ETF – Fund Overview. 2025.

data.title: BigBear.Ai: Strategic Shift Emerging, Execution Still Unproven (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
BigBear.ai (BBAI) has transitioned from a valuation-excess stage in October 2025, when I issued a Sell call. The share prices are down ~40% since, and I think it is a time to book profits and move to a Hold. The warrant overhang is a lesser mechanical pressure now. The risks of further dilutive events have shifted from existential funding to more growth investments needs. An Ask Sage acquisition also makes ~20% revenue uptick possible in 2026.

BigBear is still not a Buy because execution and integration risks remain. As of now, we do not have any proof of a rebound in core revenue. The profitability situation has also remained where it was in October. Also, a lack of early stage traction does not rule out further multiple contraction.

Growth and Profitability Concerns Stay

With Q3 data in, the revenue contraction issues are still somewhat at play. Somewhat, because there hasn't been further sequential decline. The guidance for the full year is a wide range from $125m-$140m and that is below the TTM revenue. Core organic revenue growth is missing - for an early stage defense-focused AI contractor, the proof of concept is often lumpy and requires years of build and months of sales cycle. BigBear had also run into hurdles like an untimely government shutdown, further delaying any possible traction. However, fact is, traction so far is not strong enough to call the product-market fit validated yet. That's the bigger concern - actual revenue trends matter less at this stage, but de-growth signs do not help BigBear's cause.

The street already embeds a 20-25% growth in 2026. It will be important to see how BigBear guides ahead in the upcoming Q4 quarterly call and whether this estimate gets an upgrade. Organic growth remains as critical as the overall growth outlook for the next year. As of now, the ~$30m revenue uptick in 2026, broadly matches with the ~$25m ARR from the Ask Sage acquisition. Integration timelines and organic growth beyond the Ask Sage addition will be critical to gauge.

The significant EBITDA losses I had noted in October also persist in Q3. Adjusted EBITDA was reported as -$9.4m against $900k in Q3 2024. There is no sign of any near term margin inflection either. Gross margins declined. Margin improvements ahead will depend on SG&A efficiencies post-integration and growth investment outlook.

Some Strategic Changes

The revenue and profitability trends don't inspire an upgrade, because there is only a pause in terms of further sequential decline. Where the upgrade to a Hold starts emerging is a potential business model transformation at play. Bigbear's earlier profile was that of a AI-enabled defense contractor - that is, lumpy and project based revenue, modest margins and a services-heavy mix. The Ask Sage acquisition introduces recurring revenue. The integration risks aside, this is going to immediately impact revenue by ~20%, mostly in a recurring format that smooths the overall revenue profile.

The magnitude is not a game changer yet, per se, but there are a few factors that call for watching the synergy benefits ahead. For one, the ~$25m ARR is already a six-fold increase in Ask Sage's topline over the past year alone. The growth is visibly strong here and even if it slows ahead, the revenue contributions could easily become material over the course of the next two years. While early stage growth dynamics are difficult to predict and do not necessarily scale at same multiples, a 2x jump next year and that followed by say 50% in the second year alone, takes revenue contributions to ~$75m annually- that is more than half Bigbear's current annual revenue. This is a purely hypothetical path I have outlined just for context.

There are other factors beyond revenue impact too that need some watching. One is gross margins could be healthy in this segment and that could mean overall better gross margins for BigBear - something the next few quarters could reveal after integration completes. Cross-selling opportunities may also open up. Also, although this may not be transformational right away, it signals a strategic shift. The revenue profile now has a recurring element in it. This could also signal a strategic shift to include secure GenAI infrastructure into BigBear's core - further inorganic additions (platform style acquisitions) or internal builds in this direction is not ruled out.

Dilution Concerns Lower But Exists

Another factor that looks less restrictive today is the effect of dilution and warrants. It is not yet a very optimistic capital structure story, but there are some nuanced improvements to the situation in October. Based on the Q3 Form 10-Q, the share count has increased ~73% YTD as of Q3 data (FY2025). Since Q3 though, the share count has remained stable. Some of the primary contributors toward this dilution are the ATM issuance of ~143m shares and another ~17m shares from 2029 note conversions. Around 15m shares came from warrant exercises.

The mechanical ceiling presented by the warrants is less restrictive today after share price corrections. Legacy IPO warrants carry a $11.5 strike, which is looking quite far away from the ~$4 levels BigBear is trading at today. So, it is not dilutive in the near term, unless the stock trades above the strike. In fact, the primary dilution pressures now appear to lie in convertibles, not warrants - especially the 2029 conversion, which has around $144m tied to it.

Future dilution events should be contained now though, as the cash position has drastically improved over the past couple of quarters. This is however, subject to how management wants to deploy growth investments and whether any further acquisitions in recurring platform plays (like Ask Sage) are in play. Management has remained open to fund growth through equity issuance, so capital structure may remain a relevant topic going ahead too, although the cash position looks better positioned now.

Booking Profits Ahead of Q4

The most important factor behind booking profits and moving BigBear to a watch and Hold stage is that valuations have compressed significantly from ~20x sales to around ~11x sales. Although there could be room for further compression from these levels, the Ask Sage acquisition and recurring revenue mix and a reduced dilution threat perception ahead, increase odds of a rerating ahead, after meaningful compression.

I therefore recommend booking profits on shorts initiated in October and awaiting Q4 commentary for further directions. The most important metric that I will keep an eye on is FY2025 closing revenue. The YTD revenue is ~$100m. Assuming no material sequential deterioration, i.e. ~$30m Q4 revenue, the full year revenue lands just around the midpoint of the guidance ($125m-$140m). If revenue tracks the lower end of the guidance range, that would imply further topline deceleration sequentially and encourage another phase of selling. On the other hand, if revenue comes out toward the higher end of the range, that could signal a rebound and keep the Hold stance intact.

I will also await any guidance around integration timelines for Ask Sage, and how management guides the next financial year growth. If the 2026 guidance shows some organic growth beyond inorganic addition from Ask Sage, we could evaluate a Buy too.

data.title: eToro: The Rebound Is Just Beginning As Net Inflows Rise
data.description:
Alongside the sharp fall in software stocks in 2026, the other major decliner that has driven capital flight is the sharp fall in the price of Bitcoin, which is still (at the time of writing) struggling to find support at the ~$70k mark and has lost ~30% over the past month alone.

That slide has taken down many stocks in the crypto ecosystem as well, even those whose direct exposure to falling prices isn't severe. eToro (ETOR), a brokerage company that began in Europe and has recently expanded to the U.S., is one of the major stocks that has been caught up in crypto-price pessimism, even though commissions from crypto trading only form a small chunk of its business. eToro recently jumped 20%+ after reporting strong Q4 results, though this jump only partially offsets a steep decline relative to share prices near $60 closer to the company's IPO last year. In my view, this is the beginning of a far more substantial rebound in this underappreciated company.

I last wrote a strong buy article on eToro in January, when the stock was trading in the low $30s. Note that I rarely assign a rating of "strong buy" to any stock, since to me that indicates only the companies in which I have the highest conviction from both a fundamentals and valuation perspective. Post-Q4 earnings, my conviction in eToro has only risen, especially with the company hinting at an expanding product flywheel for 2026 that will help to accelerate net fund inflows, further diversifying the company from any concentrated crypto risks. I remain at a strong buy rating here.

There are a couple of recent factors that investors need to keep in mind here when considering a buy on eToro. The first is that while eToro has been heavily associated with crypto trading, it has done a fantastic job at diversification recently. In Q4, its net contribution from crypto (as a reminder, net contribution is the company's trading revenue less direct trading costs) fell to just 24%, down from 50% in the year-ago quarter (and meanwhile, while eToro's revenue and net contribution fell, it fell only in the low double digits and came ahead of expectations - more details in the next section). In short, I think a large chunk of eToro's post-earnings gains is coming from investors realizing that eToro is well equipped to sustain growth and profitability even as crypto markets remain shaky.

We note as well that eToro has many product catalysts to drive growth as well. The snapshot below showcases all of the company's recent launches, as well as the products it has on the roadmap for 2026:

Several of these new features are of particular note. First, the company has been emphasizing targeting "neo banking" and savings account features on a market-by-market basis. For example, in France the company launched retirement accounts and life insurance products, and in the UK it added support from ISAs (individual savings accounts). We note that these efforts have helped to grow the company's net inflows. Also noteworthy is eToro's growing focus on its App Store and allowing users to build third-party applications directly within the eToro ecosystem. Over time, we think marketplace fees can become a meaningful, recurring source of software revenue for the company.

All in all, eToro remains an incredibly compelling company. Keep riding the post-earnings rally higher.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through eToro's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 net contribution trends are shown below:

Total net contribution fell -10% y/y to $227 million. As a reminder, net contribution had an especially tough comp against heightened crypto trading activity in Q4 of FY24, owing to the surge in crypto-related assets on the promise of deregulation after Trump was re-elected to the White House. The tough comp suffered a double whammy from price volatility in the past few months, as hardened crypto investors typically take the philosophy of "HODL" (hold on for dear life) when prices decline, reducing trading activity overall. Still, we note that net contribution from equities and commodity trading grew 45% y/y to $116 million, and despite market volatility in Q4, net contribution also grew 6% sequentially from Q3. Wall Street had only expected $218 million in net contribution in the quarter, or roughly flat quarter over quarter.

What continues to impress me is that in the wake of market volatility, eToro's expanding product flywheel plus its U.S. launch continues to catalyze new deposits into the platform. Total money transfers grew 29% y/y to $3.7 billion in the quarter, with the company also adding ~80k net-new funded accounts to end the quarter with 3.81 million accounts.

And so despite the erosion in value in crypto assets, we note that net deposits have still helped eToro to grow its assets under administration by 11% y/y to $18.5 billion. A growing chunk of this AUA is in cash ($3.6 billion, +20% y/y). We note that eToro's growing cash balances have also helped the company to actually increase its net interest income (up 18% y/y to $59 million), despite the headwinds from falling interest rates.

The company continues to plan on stepping up its marketing in 2026 to aid its recent U.S. launch. Per CEO Jonathan Assia's remarks on the Q&A portion of the Q4 earnings call on the company's progress in the U.S.:

So we've been very active since the IPO in expanding eToro's U.S. product launches and product road map since then we've launched CopyTrader. We plan to launch in H1 this year, Smart Portfolios, which is based on our RIA license, which is in process. We're looking at prediction markets as well. For the U.S., we've seen a significant uptake as we started scaling some of the marketing activities from Q4 to Q1. So we are seeing a significant uptick to something that's still early stages of the U.S. business, but we're very excited about continuing to launch all of eToro's global product road map here in the U.S. I do believe that now with being able to accelerate product development, we'll be able to actually launch more of our products than we expected originally in 2026 here in the U.S.

And we're making sure we are, I'd say, selective in how we scale our marketing budget, here in the U.S., we want to see CAC to LTV, while not at the same levels as globally, we want to see that ratio between CAC to LTV increase. We've seen the best ratio we've seen to date last year, especially, by the way, moving forward into the year. And that basically gives us the ability to continue and scale up marketing activity and then funded accounts where we believe product engagement will then follow as well."

We note that healthy trading activity has continued into January. Yes, it's true that crypto trades fell -50% y/y (with average trade amounts also falling -34% y/y, implying ~$728 million in net trading activity, down ~67% y/y). But non-crypto trades rose 55% y/y to 74 million trades at an average investment of $252 per trade (+8% y/y), implying $18.65 billion in non-crypto trading activity that grew 66% y/y. We're hoping that the strength in equities activity will help investors shift their opinion on eToro and reduce its correlation to overall crypto market status.

eToro also managed $87 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q4 at a 38% margin on net contribution dollars, slightly down from 43% in the year-ago Q4. Still, we note the company managed adjusted EBITDA growth on a full-year basis to $317 million (+4% y/y).

Valuation, Risks, and Key Takeaways

Forward valuations are tough for eToro because the company doesn't provide guidance (and for good reason: it's tough to predict the markets and trading activity overall). That said, even using conservative estimates, it's clear to see that eToro remains a deep value stock.

At current share prices near $33, the company trades at a $2.77 billion market cap. Netting off $1.33 billion in cash and crypto assets against $107.8 million in deposits owed to users gives us an enterprise value of $1.55 billion.

This represents just a 4.9x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA multiple against trailing adjusted EBITDA. We expect that with new market launches, especially in the U.S., net deposits fueling net interest income, and continued strong equity trading trends, the company should be able to grow net contribution and adjusted EBITDA in FY26 (consensus is currently calling for 8% growth in net contribution levels to $936 million on a full-year basis).

Of course, there are also risks to keep in mind here. The major wildcard for eToro is its U.S. launch, where the company is up against both established brokerages as well as the rising popularity of Robinhood (HOOD). Even Coinbase (COIN), in an attempt to diversify from crypto, has also announced support for stock trading. eToro's attempts to break into the world's largest and most important investment market come as a late entrant and may not be successful.

That said, with strong deposit inflows and a recently boosted share buyback program (with $150 million now open on its authorization, or 5% of its current market cap), I see more positives than negatives for eToro. Stay long here post-earnings.

data.title: Seaboard: New Vessels Hitting The Water While Pork Remains Strong
data.description:
Introduction

While I generally don’t like companies or holding companies with a majority shareholder, it usually also means there is a stronger incentive to perform well. In Seaboard Corporation's (SEB) case, the activities are quite diversified (pork, turkey, marine services, agricultural trading services), while the focus remains on keeping the balance sheet very strong despite investing in growth initiatives. The stock is now up over 40% since I last discussed the company in February 2024.

The company recently released its financial results for FY 2025, and as its quarterly updates are usually pretty short, the full annual report provides an interesting look under the hood.

A look at the full-year results

Seaboard is active in several different segments. The pork division is a vertically integrated pork producer and is Seaboard’s most capital-intensive division, as it represents 41% of the company’s total assets and 37% of all inventory levels. The CT&M segment includes commodity trading, processing, and logistics services to the agricultural sector.

While the latter represents a total revenue of almost $5.2 billion, it’s a low-margin division with operating margins below 3%. That’s also the case for the pork segment, where the operating income in 2025 came in at $67 million on a $2.02 billion revenue, but that was an improvement from the $20 million in operating income on a $2.06 billion revenue in 2024.

The smaller divisions have higher margins. The marine segment (cargo shipping services) saw its operating margin increase to exceeding 10%, while the power segment saw a $46 million operating income on a $232 million revenue. And while that is a decrease compared to the results in 2024 and 2025, the power segment (the production of electricity in the Dominican Republic) remains an important division for Seaboard as it has earmarked a $150 million investment budget in 2026 for the construction of EDM IV, a new barge project in the Dominican Republic. The total investment in EDM IV is expected to be $315 million, and the project will be operational in 2028. The power plant will have gas and steam turbines, which will be more efficient than the older plants like EDM II. Seaboard is looking into its options to perhaps monetize EDM II once EDM IV is operational.

Taking all these elements into consideration, the total revenue came in at $9.75 billion in 2025 (and $2.4 billion in Q4), resulting in a substantial increase in its gross income, which jumped by about 20% to $693 million.

After also deducting the higher SG&A expenses, the consolidated operating income was a very respectable $239 million, a 50% higher result compared to FY 2024. Additionally, the income from affiliates (mainly the 52.5% stake in the Butterball turkey brand) also increased, resulting in a pre-tax income of $410 million and a net profit of $501 million after also recording an income tax benefit. The net profit attributable to the common shareholders of Seaboard was $496 million, which works out to an EPS of just under $515 based on the weighted average share count of just over 964,000 shares. Seaboard continues to buy back its own shares, and the current share count has decreased to just under 958,000 shares.

Looking at the company’s cash flow performance, we see a reported operating cash flow of $568 million, but this includes a net investment of $100 million in the working capital position. We should also deduct the $38 million in lease payments, resulting in an underlying operating cash flow of $630 million.

The company invested about $562 million, so its net free cash flow was relatively low, but keep in mind that the majority of the capital expenditures are geared towards further growth. After all, the total amount of depreciation and amortization expenses is less than $320 million, while the combination of capex and lease payments came in at almost twice that amount.

Seaboard disclosed that about $302 million of the cash capex was related to making down payments on vessels under construction. Six new vessels were delivered during 2025, and an additional new vessel was ordered in 2025.

Seaboard’s balance sheet remains strong. At the end of December, the company had $1.23 billion in cash and short-term investments, while its current liabilities include $458 million in line of credit debt and $977 million in long-term debt for a total net debt of around $200 million.

The stock is currently trading close to its tangible book value, but you could argue that the fair value of the investments in affiliates is higher than the $795 million book value.

Investment thesis

It will be interesting to see how the company’s marine segment performs in the current financial year, and I hope the recently delivered vessels will perform well. I don’t expect Seaboard to generate free cash flow this year as the company continues to invest hundreds of millions of dollars to increase its asset base. But with plenty of liquid assets on the balance sheet and a positive working capital position of exceeding $2 billion, Seaboard can easily take care of the investments. The dividend remains very low ($2.25 per share per quarter), and most cash flow is reinvested.

I currently have no position in Seaboard, but I am keeping an eye on the company and its progress.

data.title: Heron: Near The Bottom Of A Trading Range Despite Positives
data.description:
Heron Therapeutics (HRTX) is down over 10% year-to-date despite preliminary Q4'25 numbers triggering a rally earlier in January. I last covered HRTX in August, rating the name a hold, noting that the company had done a good job of dealing with its debt, but Zynrelef sales were fairly flat. This article takes a look at Q3'25 and preliminary Q4'25 numbers for HRTX, plus the road ahead for the company, to arrive at a buy rating.

Acute Care Franchise Performs

HRTX's Q3'25 earnings came with Zynrelef net sales of $9.3M, an initial breakout from three straight quarters of sales in the mid-to-low $8M's. At that time, the company noted it had launched a dedicated sales team for Zynrelef (Q3'25) was now selling every unit of Zynrelef as the easier-to use vial-access-needle form and as of October 1 and had a product specific J-code for Zynrelef, which streamlines reimbursement.

These tailwinds might be behind the further breakout in Zynrelef sales in Q4'25, with preliminary revenues reported on January 9 of ~$12.5M, representing 34% growth over Q3'25, an unusual jump for a drug on the market for nearly 5 years. Preliminary Q4'25 numbers also came with continued growth in Aponvie

Q3'25 net revenue Q4'25 preliminary net revenue Percent change
Zynrelef $9.3M ~$12.5M ~34%
Aponvie $3.0M ~$3.8M ~27%
Cinvanti $24.0M ~$22.9M

~-5%
Sustol $1.9M ~$1.3M ~-32%
Total $38.2M ~$40.5M ~6%

There is room for further growth in Aponvie as well, as a dedicated team only launched on July 1, 2025, and finished training in October. Further, updated post-operative nausea and vomiting (PONV) guidelines noting Aponvie could aid further recognition of the product.

In addition, the 2025 PONV prophylaxis consensus guidelines are expected to be published in Q4. APONVIE is anticipated to be part of the guidelines, which should significantly increase awareness of its availability and clinical profile. We look forward to aligning our education and disseminating the information with our recently expanded field sales and medical teams.

Mark Hensley, Executive VP & COO at HRTX, Q3'25 earnings call.

Indeed, on December 4, 2025, HRTX noted the new guidelines highlight Aponvie and its advantages.

Oncology Care Franchise Underperforming

The growth of Aponvie and Zynrelef offset declines in sales from Cinvanti and Sustol in Q4'25, with the company even speaking about discontinuing marketing of Sustol. HRTX had previously guided for a potential dip in Cinvanti sales in Q3 and Q4'25 due to competitive pressures, and that did manifest in Q4'25. The company believes the expansion of Aponvie, however, can aid Cinvanti sales.

In oncology supportive care, CINVANTI remains a steady anchor. As APONVIE expands and deepens our relationships with anesthesia and pharmacy, we see CINVANTI ordering rise in those hospitals.

Mark Hensley, Executive VP & COO at HRTX, Q3'25 earnings call.

While we already know Q4'25 Cinvanti sales did see a decline compared to Q3'25, the company might be able to report some progress to date for Q1'26 when it reports its Q4'25 earnings on February 26, 2026. It might be possible to show a recovery, or at least no further loss of sales from Cinvanti, if the Aponvie launch is helping the ordering of Cinvanti too. It should also be noted that HRTX reported numbers on average daily unit and ordering accounts for Zynrelef with Q3'25 earnings, which provides a preview for the performance in the quarter to date.

For example, on the Q3'25 conference call, analysts noted the spike in daily units of Zynrelef in early Q4'25, pointing towards 18% growth, and indeed the quarter went on to outperform for Zynrelef with ~34% net revenue growth compared to Q3'25.

Congratulations on the quarter. Just turning back to the slide with respect to ZYNRELEF in the first few weeks of October, it looks like you're pushing 18% in terms of increase. And that's on a month-over-month basis. Is that correct?

Carl Byrnes, Northland Capital Markets, Research Division

Yes, Carl, that's -- it's roughly correct. Yes, it's 17%, 18% right in there.

Mark Hensley, Executive VP & COO at HRTX, Q3'25 earnings call.

As such, those following HRTX might be able to gauge if Aponvie and Zynrelef demand growth is continuing, which would help gauge whether or not the company might be able to offset declines in sales from Sustol and perhaps Cinvanti.

Additional Financials

HRTX is set to report Q4'25 earnings on Thursday, February 26, with a conference call pre-market. Preliminary Q4'25 net revenues of ~$40.5M and full-year net revenues of $154.9M suggest a beat of current estimates ($39.5M and $153.9M), although there were no preliminary numbers on cash, expenses, net loss, etc. Current analyst estimates are for -0.02 EPS for Q4'25.

Looking at Q3'25 numbers then, HRTX reported a net loss of $17.5M for Q3'25, or -$0.10 per share, although this was impacted by an $11.3M loss due to debt extinguishment during the quarter. For Q3'25, HRTX also reported a cost of product sales of $11.9M, R&D expenses of $3.5M, G&A expense of $14.0M, and S&M expense of $12.9M. HRTX does report the non-GAAP metric of adjusted EBITDA, which was positive for Q3'25 at $1.5M, compared to -$355K for Q3'24.

HRTX finished Q3'25 with cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of $55.5M. The company has $107.5M of non-current notes payable on the balance sheet from its Working Capital Facility Agreement with Hercules, which matures on September 1, 2030. HRTX also had a further $32.2M of non-current convertible notes payable on the balance sheet from its 2031 convertible notes issuance, conducted in August 2025 as part of measures to extinguish its prior debt. I don't expect HRTX to have to raise cash soon, as the company's net cash used in operating activities was $18.4M in the first nine months of 2025, its revenues are growing, it can draw another $40M from the Working Capital Facility Agreement, and the company's debt doesn't mature near-term. Indeed, HRTX might be able to shift to reporting net incomes and consistent cash flow positivity, provided it maintains financial discipline while continuing to grow its revenues.

HRTX trades with a market cap of $218.2M ($1.19 per share).

Risks

There are multiple risks to a long position in HRTX, a few of which are noted in this section.

Firstly, while the company looks set to discontinue marketing of Sustol, the company has spoken about launching a new form in the future, such as with a narrower gauge needle. If those efforts increase R&D expenses but are not appreciated by the market, then the impact on the stock would likely be negative.

Further, while sales declines from Sustol are likely built in, with the company reporting several quarters of declines and planning to discontinue marketing of the product, further declines from CINVANTI are likely not built into the stock. As such, if further declines in CINVANTI net revenues are seen, HRTX's stock could experience a particularly pronounced tumble, as CINVANTI is still the biggest earner.

Lastly, HRTX could engage in business development, such as acquiring a new product to add to its pipeline, but if the market doesn't view any such deal as being on good terms or is unsure if the product is a good fit, then the stock could tumble.

Conclusions

While the continued decline of Sustol is disappointing, it is currently being offset by increases in Aponvie and Zynrelef sales, with a lot of the damage from the decline in sales of Sustol done now. Any insights on whether or not Cinvanti can at least remain approximately stable going forward will be important for 2026, but I don't feel there is any evidence there of a Sustol-like decline/capitulation; instead, we are just seeing a bit of sideways action from a therapeutic that hasn't really been growing for a while now. Despite the positive update in January that led to HRTX rallying towards $1.50, the stock now finds itself below $1.20, which, given the breakout performance of Zynrelef, might provide a buying opportunity (I'm long HRTX). I think the company can continue to perform with each earnings in 2026, as the hit from Sustol's decline is likely built in, Cinvanti will likely hold up fine, and Zynrelef/Aponvie will continue to grow. I currently rate HRTX a buy.

data.title: The Next Sector That Could Be Cannibalized By AI
data.description:
After the "SaaS Apocalypse" and the disruption caused by AI that triggered heavy selling in software stocks, I still haven't seen a detailed analysis of the next industry that could be cannibalized. I'm talking about the HCM (Human Capital Management) sector, which, I believe, could be the next target of AI's disruption. At the end of the day, as with any technological transformation, there will be winners and losers. But in this sector, for now, everyone appears to be losing due to an uncertain outlook.

My personal experience briefly connected me to human resource management within public institutions. One of the roles I held during my professional career involved screening resumes, analyzing between 50 and 100 applications per day. At times, I also coordinated initial 30- to 45-minute interviews, which weren't yet fully virtual in 2019. Looking back at those tasks, it's easy to understand the automation revolution we are experiencing today. A well-trained AI system can analyze 1000 resumes in just seconds, identify the top 10 best candidates based on the required skills, and even schedule interviews through a chatbot or advanced AI agent.

That's just one example of automation. Many human tasks could disappear or become significantly simplified. For example, a study suggests that between 60% and 70% of current L&D could be fully automated with AI, while overall workflows in the sector may require up to 30% fewer staff. HCM companies such as Automatic Data Processing (ADP) and Paylocity Holding Corporation (PCTY) charge on a per-employee basis for access to their platforms. If AI enables a company to operate with 50 employees instead of 100, the per-employee revenue for an HCM provider is effectively cut in half. A positive view might suggest that this greater efficiency will improve and make the sector more profitable, but in my opinion, this will not be the prevailing outcome when a significant part of the revenue model depends directly on employee headcount.

Based on my limited personal experience in this sector, along with a detailed analysis of the market, I'll try to explain my thesis on how AI could claim a new victim. It doesn't mean there will be no opportunities in the segment. In fact, a few months ago I recommended Asure Software (ASUR) as a long-term buy, and, although cautiously, I maintain that position. Prior to the SaaS Apocalypse gaining traction, ASUR had delivered gains of approximately 24%. However, the broader software industry was clearly not immune to the disruptive impact of AI. Here are the declines of major software companies so far this year:

Software Fears Following The Launch Of OpenAI And Anthropic Models

The risk of AI increasingly replacing tasks within the software industry intensified with the release of OpenAI's (OPENAI) and Anthropic's (ANTHRO) coding models, GPT-5.3 Codex and Claude Opus 4.6, respectively. These models are capable of continuously executing complex tasks with minimal human intervention, potentially rendering many traditional software functions obsolete.

This shift triggered a new allocation of capital. A clear example of this, in my view, is the decline of the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV), whose top holdings include Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Palantir Technologies (PLTR), Oracle Corporation (ORCL), Salesforce (CRM), Intuit (INTU), and AppLovin Corporation (APP), which together account for roughly 43% of the fund. IGV is down 21% so far in 2026. The other side of the coin is the Invesco AI and Next Gen Software ETF (IGPT), which is more heavily weighted towards companies such as Micron Technology (MU), Meta Platforms (META), Alphabet (GOOG), and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). IGPT is up 3.8% to date in 2026.

Perhaps a 3.8% gain may not seem significant at first glance. However, when compared with other software companies and to the SPY itself, it suggests a clear capital rotation. That said, the software universe is broad, encompassing thousands of industries that operate in very different ways. For some large, established technology firms, such as Microsoft, this correction may not represent a structural threat. On the contrary, it could present a buying opportunity.

In fact, I took advantage of this recent fall to add to my position. I believe Microsoft has a very solid business in Azure cloud services, delivering enviable double-digit annual growth given its scale. Moreover, its significant capital investment, viewed by many analysts as a potential risk, is, in my view, one of its key strengths. It reinforces the idea that the company will remain a central player in the development of AI.

However, there are other industries, perhaps less essential or more exposed, that could indeed become vulnerable to the next generation of AI. In my view, sectors such as IT services, financial intermediation, and research and consulting may face significant challenges in adapting to this shift.

The HCM Industry

In that context, the HCM segment could face significant threats. This is something investors already appear to be pricing in, reducing exposure before it becomes clear who will successfully adapt and who will not. From my perspective, it is always better to experience moments of uncertainty from the outside.

In the following chart you can see the main HCM companies by market capitalization, grouped in this comparison. The exceptions are ASUR and Workday (WDAY). I included ASUR because it has been my only investment in the sector, as I explained earlier. And I included Workday because, while reviewing the earnings presentations of these companies, I noticed that several of them referenced Workday in their comparisons. And it does make sense because, despite not being a staffing or recruitment company, it still competes for the same client base as many of its peers. Workday is generally classified within the application software industry as it provides a cloud-based platform specializing in HCM solutions.

Beyond the operational exceptions within the comparison, what stands out is that none of these companies reported weak results in their latest earnings releases. As was the case with companies like Microsoft, which delivered strong results and even better future forecasts, the same thing happened with the HCM companies.

Next, I'll make a brief analysis of the main companies that, although they may have some different approaches, are all focused on human capital management and could be affected by AI-driven disruption.

Analysis Of Leading HCM Companies

WDAY will present its Q4 2024 results on February 24th, and the latest revisions for the quarter show 23 upwards and 11 downwards compared to EPS projections. The analyst consensus expects earnings per share of $2.32, which would represent a 20% YoY increase, and revenue of $2.52 billion, up 14% YoY. That level of revenue growth is below its five-year average by 17%.

Even so, it still represents double-digit growth. In the last 90 days, of the 43 Wall Street analysts who analyzed the company, 23 have rated it a Strong Buy, 7 a Buy, and 17 a Hold. The stock has received no sell ratings in the last 90 days. Over the same period, the shares have fallen 38%, in line with the broader industry's pessimism.

ADP reported its Q2 FY26 results on January 29, exceeding consensus estimates for both revenue (+0.4%) and earnings per share, which came in at $2.62 versus the expected $2.57. Revenue increased 6% YoY in the quarter, while the adjusted EBIT margin expanded by 80 bps to 25.8%, representing a 10% YoY rise. At first glance, there appear to be no profitability issues. Even more: ADP's EBIT margin significantly exceeds the industry average of 10.5%.

Meanwhile, forward growth expectations remained unchanged in the latest earnings presentation. Some concerns were raised during the call regarding the decline in PEO margins, which could serve as an early warning sign of AI-related pressure. However, once again, this shouldn't necessarily compromise profitability in the medium term. Adjusted EPS outlook for fiscal year 2026 was revised upward from 8%-10% to 9%-10%, supported by the company's share repurchase program. There is something else: over the past three months there have been 14 upward revisions to the fiscal year EPS estimates and no downward revisions.

Paychex (PAYX) is another major player in the industry. The most recent reported results correspond to Q2 FY26, released last November. The company is scheduled to report again at the end of March, with consensus estimates pointing to double-digit YoY growth of 18% in revenue and 12% in EPS. PAYX also exceeded revenue and EPS expectations, while its revenue grew 17.4% YoY, the highest quarterly growth in the past two years.

Last month, the company announced an expansion of its share repurchase program, increasing it from $400 million to $1 billion. During 2025, PAYX returned $1.5 billion to shareholders through dividends and buybacks. PAYX has an excellent dividend profile rating, with an expected annual yield of 4.5%. Earnings estimate revisions remain positive for the fiscal year, with 14 upward revisions and only one downward.

Kanzhun Limited (BZ) also reported earnings in March, with an estimation of a double-digit YoY growth of 18% in revenue and 21.8% in EPS. The company has authorized a $250 million share repurchase program through August 2026. However, its dividend profile lags behind that of PAYX and ADP, with a yield of 0.97%. The stock delivered strong performance in 2025, rising 52.3%, making it the fastest-growing company in the human resources and employment segment last year. By comparison, the S&P 500 gained 18.2% over the same period.

Paycom Software (PAYC) reported Q4 FY25 earnings last week, delivering EPS in line with expectations and revenue slightly ahead of estimates by 0.3%. Revenue for the quarter grew 10% YoY, while gross margin expanded by 80 basis points compared to last year, reaching 83.9%. The problem I perceive at PAYC is the projected slowdown in revenue growth for 2026. Management guided for sales growth of 6%-7%, below the 8.5%-9% anticipated by analysts.

Paylocity Holding Corporation (PCTY) reported Q2 FY26 earnings on February 5, delivering a 15.9% EPS surprise. Revenue for the fiscal year ending in July is expected to grow approximately 10%, which maintains the trend observed so far: double-digit growth. That said, PCTY does show signs of gradual deceleration, with revenue growth of 15% in FY25 and 17% in FY24. Over the past five years, the company has posted a revenue CAGR of 18%, which remains solid by industry standards. Overall this reinforces the core idea of my thesis: I still don't see clear signs of significant profitability deterioration.

Overall, I found that the companies analyzed continue to show stable revenue trends, consistent earnings beats or in-line results versus consensus, solid profitability metrics, and active capital return programs through share repurchases and dividends. Despite these fundamentals, all of them have experienced significant share price declines so far in 2026. Why? I believe this is due to the key role AI is playing in the shaping of the next generation of HCM. Less staff and less human resource management will be required, which will imply a reduction in business models based on employee fees.

And part of that impact is already materializing. No technological shift unfolds overnight. Even before OpenAI and Anthropic launched their AI coding models, revenue growth expectations across the industry were already showing signs of deceleration. Below, you can see how consensus expectations indicate a slowdown in future growth (or moderation, at best) compared to the historical 3-year CAGR.

Valuation: Beware Of The Allure Of Cheap Stocks

In line with recent pessimism surrounding the impact of AI on the HCM industry, sector valuations now have lower P/E ratios than they did six months ago.

Paycom is currently trading at 15x earnings, while six months ago it was trading 47% higher. Workday, which, as I've explained before, I considered for its digital infrastructure and its membership in the software application industry, typically commands higher valuation multiples than its HCM peers. However, its P/E ratio has also compressed significantly, declining by approximately 42% over the same period.

It is understandable that some analysts may begin to label certain names in the sector as bargains. Compared to six months ago, that's not true. However, with the disruption caused by AI, we now need to take a closer look. It can be tempting to focus on valuation multiples that appear low relative to both the sector average and a company's own recent historical range, especially when the underlying fundamentals still reflect revenue growth, solid profitability, stable business models, and attractive dividend profiles. Yet the key question is whether those multiples are discounting temporary pessimism or a structural shift in the industry's long-term earnings power.

A quick reading might be, "There is no reason for this stock to suffer such punishment; this is a buying opportunity". That reaction is common in financial markets, even in my own analyses. And after a while, what may initially appear to be a technical bottom can quickly prove otherwise if market sentiment and structural risks are not properly assessed. What looks like an overreaction can evolve into another crash. That's why I recommend being more cautious in this sector from now on. I don't recommend participating in massive sell-offs because there's still much to understand about how each company adapts. I recommend a hold position instead.

While buying on dips and selling at peaks can be tempting (you know, buy low, sell high), in finance it's not that simple. Either decision can be detrimental if the decline deepens, just as opportunities can be missed if a stock's upward momentum still has room to run.

Summary

I have tried to explain my view on HCM, a sector that I was involved in during my professional career. I also recommended buying a small company in the sector, Asure Software, and until now it wasn't my investment. In fact, that changed in early 2026, when fears about AI disruption reached the human capital and employment industry.

I also tried to explain some of the details behind those fears and their impact on the software industry as a whole, especially following the release of intelligent language models with minimal human intervention from OpenAI and Anthropic. These models are generating code faster and more efficiently than expected, which could replace several functions within software companies.

This launch accelerates spillover effects into other industries that rely on routine work and organizational management, such as HCM. Almost all the companies in the sector suffered significant sell-offs so far in 2026. If AI turns into the reality of what already seems likely (for example, the reduction of specialized personnel in outsourced human capital management), it could impact the earnings and profitability of these companies. The impact won't necessarily be the same for everyone. Some companies can quickly adapt and take advantage of modernization, but that could take a while. Those that delay too long, I believe, could be cannibalized by AI.

data.title: IREN: Major ARR Uplift Incoming
data.description:
IREN (IREN) exceeded bottom-line estimates for Q2’26 earlier this month, but the AI infrastructure company missed on revenue, leading to a sell-off from which shares have not yet recovered. IREN executed well its growth plans and is on track to deploy 140,000 GPUs by the end of the year, including the latest Blackwell chips, and the pivot from Bitcoin mining to AI Cloud services is in full swing.

The rapidly evolving Cloud computing platform reported strong growth in its core business, and it is set to grow to $3.4B in annual recurring revenues by the end of this year. However, this ARR is achieved based off only a small portion of the company's secured power, meaning the company has significant potential to massively grow its ARR in the next several years... if strong CapEx trends remain intact. I like IREN’s business development, even more so after shares dropped following the Q2’26 report. The correction creates an engagement opportunity, especially for investors that see a continual multi-year GPU upgrade cycle ahead and have a high-risk tolerance.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of IREN a strong buy in my November 2025 work, Microsoft Deal Is A Game-Changer (Rating Upgrade) -- mainly because the company was successfully transitioning from a Bitcoin cryptocurrency-oriented mining operation to an integrated AI Cloud platform. The NeoCloud has been aggressively growing its Data Center footprint in North America (as detailed in November) and is on track to seriously boost its annual recurring revenue business in the years ahead, in part because of the opening of new AI Data Center campuses. I like the overall investment setup and believe that shares have considerable long-term potential to grow into a higher valuation.

IREN’s Q2’26 Vs. Estimates

IREN reported better-than-expected bottom-line results, but the company missed on revenue estimates, unfortunately triggering a sell-off: the AI infrastructure enterprise reported non-GAAP EPS of $(0.03), which beat the average prediction by a massive $0.27. The top-line figure was $184.7M, which fell short of Wall Street’s consensus estimate by $39.6M.

IREN generated consolidated revenues of $184.7M, a 23% Q/Q decline. As noted previously, the company is still involved in cryptocurrency mining, which generated $167.4M in revenue, showing a contraction of 28% Q/Q. This segment remains inherently volatile as the AI infrastructure player pivots its power capacity towards AI infrastructure.

In contrast, AI Cloud Services are booming: they reached $17.3M, up 137% Q/Q, in part because the platform has started to execute on the massive Microsoft (MSFT) deal that was signed in November of last year.

Key to the company's growth is the buildout of its AI Data Center infrastructure. The AI Cloud Services company has secured more than 460 megawatts of secured power so far, which supports the firm’s expected GPU ramp to 140,000 units by the end of 2026.

IREN also confirmed its GPU deployment goal as it expects to execute on its Microsoft commitments as the year progresses. As IREN's 5-year $9.7B deal with Microsoft ramps up this year, management expects the phased delivery of Nvidia (NVDA)'s GB300 NVL72 liquid-cooled systems that are critical to Microsoft's AI-focused growth strategy within Azure.

The GPU deployment target includes both H100/H200 and other Blackwell chips. IREN is still on track to achieve about $3.4B in annualized run-rate revenues by the end of 2026, thereby confirming management's growth plan from last year.

Massive Potential for ARR Uplift

According to recent projections made by Mitsubishi UFJ, a leading Japanese investment bank, CapEx spending this year alone is set to reach $600B... with all major hyperscalers, including Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), and Microsoft (MSFT), set to massively ramp up their AI spending allocations.

Amazon announced a $200B CapEx program as the Cloud service provider is going all-in on growth in its AWS business. Alphabet guided for up to $185B in CapEx spending this year alone, which means investment spending is set to almost double compared to last year.

IREN has a unique opportunity for a massive ARR uplift -- an expansion of its revenue backlog -- due to massive investments in its Data Center footprint. As mentioned above, the company is on track to grow its annual recurring revenue business from $2.3B at the end of last year to an expected $3.4B as of the end of 2026.

The ~50% increase in ARR is mainly related to the delivery and installation of new Nvidia GB300 systems that will be deployed as part of IREN’s Microsoft contract. IREN currently has a grid-connected power capacity of over 4.5 GW, including the establishment of a new 1.6 GW Data Center campus in Oklahoma.

Since IREN only utilizes 460 MWs of power to generate $3.4B in ARR, the Data Center platform has the potential to 10X its ARR business in the next 4-5 years. If IREN scales its ARR proportionally to the deployment of secured power, the AI infrastructure company could potentially see an ARR lift to more than $33.0B by the end of the decade, assuming that GPU compute demand doesn't dry up and that IREN can fully rent out its Data Centers.

IREN’s Valuation

Shares of IREN are trading at a significantly lower price-to-revenue ratio following the Q2 post-earnings sell-off than they did before the announcement on February 5, 2026... which creates an engagement opportunity for investors that have a favorable view on CapEx spending in the Data Center industry and the demand outlook for GPUs. Shares of IREN are now trading at a forward (FY 2027) P/S ratio of 4.9X.

Other AI Cloud companies, like CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS), have similar plans to IREN for massive expansion as the sector is building out its Data Centers at an increasingly aggressive pace. CoreWeave and Nebius are top AI Cloud platforms and currently trade at price-to-revenue ratios of 2.6X and 2.8X. Nebius itself just reached a major inflection point in its core business and reported its first quarterly adjusted EBITDA profit.

In my opinion, IREN is deserving of a premium multiplier in the neocloud business because the AI infrastructure company is seeing one of the most significant ARR uplifts in the industry due to soaring demand for AI GPU compute. I believe IREN could trade at a ~6X P/S ratio going forward, under the condition that the company maintains its overall AI services growth momentum and gradually moves towards profitability.

IREN already traded at a higher than 6.0X P/S ratio not that long ago (see chart below), and I believe that the firm could return to this valuation range if CapEx spending trends and hyperscaler-driven GPU demand prove resilient in the years ahead. This forward fair value P/S implies a fair value price for IREN of $50/share, which calculates to 22% upside.

Risks With IREN

The biggest risk that I see for IREN, as well as for other neoclouds, is that they have a lot of risk acquiring GPUs, which will likely have to get up-graded over time as new, higher-performing chips come out to handle AI workloads. This makes neoclouds fundamentally reliant on a small number of hyperscalers that are responsible for the majority of GPU deployments.

If hyperscalers like Microsoft were to scale back their AI deployments, neoclouds like IREN would have trouble rolling over expiring contracts, leading, potentially, to severe underutilization of their Data Centers. What would more specifically change my view and outlook on IREN is if the infrastructure enterprise were to miss its GPU deployment goals this year or if it suffered a drop in the rate of its ARR expansion.

Closing Thoughts

The GPU-as-a-Service Cloud computing firm said it is on track to deploy 140,000 GPUs by the end of 2026, targeting a massive $3.4B in annual recurring revenue by the end of the year. This means that the company is shooting for an about 50% year-over-year increase in its annual recurring business. While the rate of ARR growth is impressive, the market may still underestimate the future revenue and ARR uplift that comes from the firm's Data Center expansion and growing power base: if IREN can fully contract its 4.5 GW power base until the end of the decade, based off current ARR, the AI Cloud platform could be on track for a massive ~10X ARR uplift, potentially leading to a massive revaluation of the firm's share price as well.

data.title: Wheaton Precious Metals: Wait For Risk Clarification
data.description:
In line with the continued attractiveness of precious metals, the Canadian miner Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) has had a good start to 2026, with over a 20% increase YTD. This rise put WPM in the same league as other big gold miners like Newmont Corporation (NEM) and Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) and much further ahead than Barrick Mining Corporation (B) (see chart below).

Positive as the price trend so far is, when I last checked on the stock in September 2025, its elevated market multiples were a source of concern. With a price rise of 35% since, could they still be a risk to consider that can drag WPM back, despite the clear positives for it? Here, I assess the balance of the risks and positives of WPM to determine what's next.

4 positives for Wheaton Precious Metals

Recent developments indicate that there's much to like about Wheaton. Its production and sales for 2025 look good, and its outlook for 2026 is positive too, leading to attractive market multiples. All these points are highlighted below.

#1. Gold production exceeds expectations

While the company's full-year 2025 financial results are due only in the second week of March, its recently released production update for 2025 bodes well. Gold production exceeded the company's guidance range midpoint by 12.7% and the upper end by 6.9%. Moreover, gold sales' volumes touched 98.7% of the production, a notable jump from the 87.7% ratio seen in 2024. With gold contributing to 64% of the company's revenues in the first nine months of 2025 (9m 2025), this is a positive indication.

Silver, which brings in much of the remaining revenue, didn't do badly either. Its production was almost at the top end of the guidance range (see table above). Much like gold, silver too saw a big jump in the sales-to-production ratio to 88.2% (2024: 75%).

#2. Positive outcomes are foreseen in 2026 too

At the midpoint of the company's guidance for 2026, gold could see a marginal decline in production (see Table 1 below). If production continues to exceed guidance, though, sales could still throw a nice surprise.

But even if production comes in at the midpoint, gold revenues can still see a good rise considering the jump expected in gold prices. As Table 2 below shows, WPM expects the gold price to be at $4,800 per ounce in 2026. For reference, in 9m 2025, the realised price was $3,191 per ounce, so a 50% price upside is forecast. The company bases the price forecast on the average seen earlier in the year and warns that it might not be realised, but other forecasters are even more bullish.

Silver's production is expected to rise by ~25% at the midpoint of the guidance range. Its price rise is also expected to be a much bigger 126%, to $80 per ounce, compared to the $35.4 per ounce seen for 9m 2025. Here too, there could be further upside. Goldman Sachs, for example, sees silver between $85 and $100 an ounce this year. Other metals like palladium and cobalt are expected to be on a similar positive trajectory in terms of both production and price in 2026 as well, as Tables 1 and 2 above show.

#3. Positive financial forecasts

For 9m 2025 alone, WPM has reported solid revenue growth of 60.4% YoY, and with an even bigger increase in adjusted net earnings of 85.4% YoY, the adjusted net margin is a massive 56.4%. As per analysts estimates on Seeking Alpha, this growth is only expected to accelerate in the full year, increasing by over 70%. Adjusted EPS is expected to make even bigger gains, as it's set to double (9m 2025: 85.2% YoY).

Based on the company's production and price projections for this year, I estimate that the revenue will do even better in 2026, almost doubling from 2025 to $4.3 billion. If the adjusted net margin remains the same as in 9m 2025, the net income figure would come to $2.4 billion. With the number of shares unchanged, the adjusted EPS would land at $5.36, representing a 1.9x increase from the expected level in 2025.

#4. Attractive forward P/E for 2026

Even though the stock has run up a lot recently, resulting in a forward non-GAAP P/E of 52.7x for 2025 compared with its five-year average of 36.7x, the number still looks competitive for 2026 at 26.6x. This suggests that over a 35% increase is possible in WPM's price this year.

It does bear mentioning, though, that its P/E for 2026 is higher than that for both AEM and NEM, at 15.3x and 13x, respectively. On the other hand, though, both AEM and NEM also have lower five-year average ratios at 21.7x and 18.1x, respectively. At a positive time for precious metals, like right now, WPM may still continue to rise. But if investors become more selective, say due to a plateauing or decline in gold and silver prices, lower valuations for peers suggest that they can have a bigger edge. For now, though, that's tomorrow's problem.

Risk from acquisition

There's a risk to the largely happy picture for 2026, though. It comes from the company's recent acquisition of a silver stream through its partnership with the Australian multi-commodity miner BHP (BHP). With this acquisition, WPM will get 33.75% of the silver produced at the Peruvian Antamina mine, bringing its total claim on the mine's production to 67.5%, as it already has a similar agreement with Glencore (OTCPK:GLNCY).

It's not immediately clear whether the purchase is baked into the company's production outlook, as both updates were released on the same day. So it's best to consider the potential downside. The $4.3 billion purchase will be paid through both cash in hand and debt. A sum of $1.9 billion is expected to be paid in cash.

The mine will also immediately add 6 million ounces to its silver production this year and for the next four years. At its forecast price of $80 per ounce of silver in 2026, the company can then expect $480 million of revenues from the stream. Assuming that its net profit margin remains at 56.4%, a profit of $271 million can be expected. This would reduce the net cash payout to $1.63 billion.

If the cash payout figure is factored into adjusted net income estimates, they would drop to $0.8 billion, and the adjusted EPS would drop to $1.75. This results in a forward P/E of over 80x. This wouldn't be a challenge at any other time if a bounce back in EPS could be reasonably expected in 2027.

But silver prices have run up a lot already. Over the past year, the silver bullion tracker fund iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) is up by over 121%. And while further price increases are expected for now, it remains to be seen how far they can sustain beyond 2026, as silver still trades at near all-time highs.

What's next?

With this risk to WPM, I'd be cautious in making an investment in the stock right now. It would be a good idea to wait until the company can provide more clarity on the acquisition's impact on earnings, as any weakness in silver price from 2027 also risks stretched valuations in the medium term as well. Either the full-year earnings release or the earnings call would be ideal to look out for in this regard.

It would be only a technicality if the impact isn't included as part of the adjusted EPS estimate, but even that could still have a bearing on sentiment and the stock's price. Along with increased production and high precious metal prices, the prospects for WPM may well continue to look good then. But that remains to be seen. For now, I'm maintaining a Hold rating.

data.title: Harding Loevner International Developed Markets Equity Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
What's on Our Minds

For most of the past 15 years, US equities didn't just outperform, they made their outperformance feel inevitable. That's what the idea of "US exceptionalism" became in practice: exceptional earnings growth, exceptional valuations (that didn't matter), and eventually, exceptional concentration of market returns. Which has increasingly narrowed to a heavy bet on AI.

A whole generation of investors has been weaned on US stock market exceptionalism. On a rolling three-year basis, the most recent stretch of US outperformance has lasted approximately 193 months, or more than 15 years—more than two-and-a-half times longer than any prior episode. Such investors may assume that the underperformance of US equities in 2025 was an aberration soon to be reversed, but market structure has changed from 15 years ago. While valuations of US and international developed markets were once similar, the US now trades at nearly twice the multiples of foreign developed stocks. The valuation gap has widened, in part, due to the rising market values of the largest "AI plays" in the US that represent a larger proportion of the overall market. By year-end 2025, the top 10 US stocks by market capitalization represented roughly 40% of the MSCI USA Index, up from about 18% in 2009. Outside the US, however, concentration slightly declined, with the top 10 members of the MSCI World ex US moving from roughly 13% to 12% of the index's value.

That widening gap in valuations and market diversity matters because it changes what US investors are currently pricing in and ultimately betting on. At today's starting point, continued US market leadership increasingly depends on a single premise: that AI will produce an extended expansion of growth and profitability, most of it captured by a select few US companies.

But AI isn't a monolithic trade; it's a capital-expenditure regime with two distinct camps of participants, and two very different risk profiles. On the demand side are the AI capex investors: hyperscalers and platform companies committing large portions of cash flow to build compute capacity at scale. On the supply side are the physical enablers of AI: the global ecosystem that makes its buildout possible, spanning chip foundries, memory-chip makers, equipment manufacturers, as well as providers of power management, cooling, and the specialized industrial infrastructure required to deploy and operate data centers.

Despite the size of NVIDIA (NVDA), the US equity market is disproportionately weighted toward the first group: the hyperscalers and the AI platform cohort. Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon.com (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), and Microsoft (MSFT) are the largest buyers of AI computing power, committing an increasing share of their cash flow to data centers and infrastructure. Their cumulative spend is widely expected to reach hundreds of billions of dollars over the next several years. This is the heart of the US AI narrative: if you believe large capex inevitably translates into even larger monetization, the US currently dominates those profit pools.

International markets, by contrast, are more heavily tilted toward the second group: the ecosystem of manufacturers and infrastructure providers. While the US-listed NVIDIA may dominate accelerator economics, it's a fabless chipmaker, meaning it can't deliver a single graphics processing unit (GPU) unless the likes of Taiwan's TSMC fabricates it. Amazon, Meta, or Microsoft may want the biggest data centers with unparalleled computing capabilities, but not one gets built without power-management systems or components from the likes of Schneider Electric (SBGSF). The US AI leaders' products and services sit atop a deeply international supply chain—foundries, memory, semiconductor equipment, precision manufacturing, and the power and thermal systems required to run AI models at scale. In other words, non-US companies may include fewer of the headline AI platforms, but are key physical enablers of the buildout.

Although the AI bubble question will continue to be asked in 2026, there isn't a simple answer to the question. Businesses investing in AI infrastructure need to build customer bases and generate enough revenue to justify their investments. If that revenue falls short of expectations, that's bad not just for those companies but also their suppliers, for while AI-related investment demand won't disappear overnight, it could slow. If that were to happen, the pain will show up first in the most cyclical parts of the industry: semiconductor equipment and certain parts of the manufacturing chain, then foundries and component suppliers, and only later in the more insulated platform economics.

Both US and international investors have a stake in AI's growth. But if the AI cycle slows, at the stock level the US market faces a double sensitivity: rich starting valuations and a large share of index returns that hinge on a narrow cohort. Therefore, the US market is more dependent on AI continuing to surprise to the upside to justify its premium. Non-US markets would not be immune, particularly the aforementioned group of more cyclical suppliers, but they start from cheaper valuations and possess a more diverse set of growth opportunities wholly unrelated to AI.

In several cases, share prices lagged even as underlying earnings continued to grow, an important reminder that the pace of AI adoption and the trajectory of AI equity returns aren't necessarily synchronized in the short run.

This is also where policy becomes more than a footnote. When valuations are demanding and market leadership is narrow, policy volatility carries a higher price. Fifteen years ago, the US was more exceptional in terms of being a haven for global capital. Then, it enjoyed an AAA sovereign credit rating and ranked top ten globally in the US-based Heritage Foundation's Index of Economic Freedom, reflecting a policy environment broadly supportive of capital formation and business activity. By 2025, the US fell to 26th on that list. Elevated uncertainty around tariffs, regulation, fiscal direction, and monetary policy doesn't need to completely derail the AI story to matter; it simply needs to raise risk premiums and widen the range of outcomes for the most crowded part of the market.

So how do we respond as investors, especially when diversification has felt like a penalty at times?

In our portfolio, we own many companies that are direct beneficiaries of the AI buildout: semiconductor manufacturing leaders and equipment suppliers, and companies exposed to the power and thermal infrastructure AI requires. These include

TSMC (TSM), the world's largest semiconductor foundry; Samsung Electronics, the leading memory producer; semiconductor manufacturing equipment suppliers such as ASML (ASML), Disco Corp (DSCSY), Lasertec (LSRCY), and Daifuku (DFKCY); and companies addressing AI's substantial power and thermal management needs, including Schneider Electric. These holdings have participated meaningfully in the upside of AI-related investment, while giving us exposure to the critical layer outside US mega-caps. The above-mentioned companies, representing a sixth of our portfolio would be considered "AI plays" by market pundits—roughly a third what the US index bet is, but above what our international developed markets benchmark holds.

We've also sought more diverse ways to participate in AI and digitization beyond the most cyclical parts of the supply chain, with software and services businesses that are applying AI to improve their products, automate processes, and expand margins. Results here were mixed in 2025. In several cases, share prices lagged even as underlying earnings continued to grow, an important reminder that the pace of AI adoption and the trajectory of AI equity returns aren't necessarily synchronized in the short run.

The more useful question isn't whether an AI bubble exists but rather where expectations are most stretched—and which stocks bear the greatest downside if the capex cycle cools. And the more actionable question is, where is the market's dependence concentrated, and what is already priced in? US exceptionalism has become tightly linked to an AI capex regime, and the US index is unusually exposed to concentrated bets facing a wide range of outcomes. International equities, meanwhile, offer participation in the same structural buildout through a greater variety of business models, often at more attractive valuations, with less single-factor concentration, and with a wider range of non-AI sources of growth.

If AI delivers on its promise, global winners will continue to extend well beyond US mega-caps. And if the capex cycle proves bumpier than the market expects, diversification across the chain and across regions may matter more than it has in years.

Market Snapshot

  • The MSCI World ex US index outpaced the MSCI USA index in the quarter and for the full year. Cheaper starting valuations, tariff and policy uncertainty in the US, and a decline in the US dollar supported better returns for international equities.
  • Canada outperformed this quarter, as Financials there benefited from an improving yield curve, and gold and silver mining stocks continued to surge. Pacific ex Japan lagged for both the quarter and the year, dragged down by poor returns in Australia and New Zealand.
  • Health Care rebounded on strong performance from pharmaceutical and biotechnology stocks. Sentiment improved following agreements between several companies and the Trump administration to provide discounted drugs to the Medicaid program, reducing policy uncertainty. Utilities also outperformed, particularly in Europe, supported by rising data-center power needs and increased grid investment.
  • Communication Services declined, dragged down by media and entertainment.
  • The cheapest quintile of stocks outperformed the most expensive quintile by over 13 percentage points. For the year, the performance gap between these cohorts was a staggering 41 percentage points.
