Portfolio in Focus

Turnover in the portfolio was higher in 2025, at 25% nearly double the average of the previous five years, as we sought to capitalize on equity-market dislocations caused by sharp swings in risk appetite. Activity in the fourth quarter was less than the prior two quarters. Relative to the beginning of the year, we lifted the portfolio's growth exposure by reducing Materials and Consumer Staples in favor of Information Technology, and added selectively to European defense following a pullback.

One such defense stock and a new holding this quarter is BAE Systems (BAESF). BAE, formerly British Aerospace, is among the largest defense contractors outside the US or China, with capabilities across air, land, sea, space, and electronic systems, and the scale to serve multiple geographies. Its intellectual property, long-standing government relationships, and track record of program execution have supported resilient profitability even through long years of restrained European defense spending. With indications of sustained higher defense budgets in Europe, alongside a push to modernize in the US, BAE's order book has strengthened while profitability has remained steady. We were able to buy shares amid a broader sell-off in European defense stocks, as investors wavered over a potential Ukraine–Russia settlement that could reduce near-term order momentum.

To fund the investments in higher-growth companies, we reduced exposure elsewhere. We sold long-standing holdings in Linde (LIN) and BHP (BHP), reducing our Materials weight to roughly 6% of the portfolio. Linde's valuation looked stretched against its high-single-digit long-term growth outlook. Meanwhile, BHP's growth outlook appeared to deteriorate, with rising Australia–China frictions increasing risks around iron ore sales to Chinese steel manufacturers, who have built ample stockpiles of ore, yet face moribund demand growth. We also sold German enterprise resource planning software provider SAP, whose demanding valuation discounts a degree of growth acceleration unlikely to materialize.

As has long been the case, our portfolio of faster-growing and higher-quality companies comes at a premium valuation, but after the last four years of crushing style headwinds, that premium sits near multi-year lows.

Overall, these changes increased the portfolio's growth profile without a meaningful sacrifice in quality, and at more reasonable valuations than a year ago. We end 2025 with a portfolio that betters the index in cash flow generation, a key driver of long-term outcomes, with a portfolio-weighted median five-year cash flow growth rate of 11.7% versus 7.9% for the index. The consensus 2026 earnings growth outlook for the portfolio of 18.5% is also materially higher than 10.6% for the index. As has long been the case, our portfolio of faster-growing and higher-quality companies comes at a premium valuation, but after the last four years of crushing style headwinds, that premium sits near multi-year lows (as shown in the chart above), offering an attractive outlook for the portfolio.

Performance and Attribution

The International Developed Markets Equity composite returned 4.9% gross of fees in the fourth quarter, not far behind the 5.3% gain in the MSCI World ex US Index. For the year, however, the composite rose 23.4% gross of fees, lagging the index return of 32.6%. The portfolio's emphasis on higher-quality companies, and consequently its light holdings of the cheapest cohort of stocks, hurt performance across the board.

Portfolio Attribution by Sector

Sector allocation was modestly helpful in the fourth quarter, particularly in Communication Services and Health Care, but stock selection detracted across several sectors.

Top contributors to relative performance:

  • Our Financials stocks bettered the index in the fourth quarter with insurer Manulife seeing a reacceleration of new business in Hong Kong Spanish commercial bank BBVA also performed strongly, with investors heartened by plans for significant share buybacks following the collapse of BBVA's bid for Banco Sabadell.
  • For the full year, we had good stock selection and a modest overweight in the top-performing IT sector, led by South Korean electronics manufacturer Samsung Electronics, Dutch semiconductor equipment manufacturer ASML, and Taiwanese semiconductor maker TSMC.
  • The portfolio's underweight in the poorly performing Consumer Discretionary sector also helped full-year performance.

Top detractors from relative performance:

  • In the fourth quarter, our Consumer Discretionary stocks lagged. Shares of Japanese media and entertainment conglomerate Sony pulled back after strong year-to-date performance, led by robust growth in its gaming and image sensors divisions.
  • For the full year, our Health Care stocks were battered by a more hostile political and regulatory environment developing in the US, the biggest profit pool for the global industry, and by further valuation compression for many of the faster-growing equipment and services companies such as Sysmex. Our hefty allocation to the sector also hurt.
  • In Industrials , Swedish multinationals Atlas Copco and Alfa Laval, along with France's Schneider Electric detracted from relative performance for the full year, with each still among the more richly priced capital goods stocks, and with growth that has wobbled amid uncertainty for capital investment outside of AI.

data.title: IREN: The Dark Before The AI Dawn
data.description:
I’ve noticed IREN (IREN) is often discussed like a pure narrative stock as investors assume will eventually work out as an AI cloud story but not one that’s expected to prove itself in the near term. Having read the earnings report I don’t think eventually is the appropriate way to think about it anymore. The demand side isn’t the issue, at least not in the way bearers think.

The issue is whether IREN can accelerate its contracted economics into reported revenues quickly enough so that its income statement stops looking like a transition and starts looking like an operating business. The distinction matters because Q2 was messy in the way transition stories are supposed to be. Revenue fell to $184.7 million from the previous quarter as Bitcoin mining revenues fell.

I was bullish on IREN back in July 2025 around at $16 per share when the AI transition was underway and after the sharp pullback since November, I believe it now qualifies as a strong buy heading into 2026. So, I remain bullish on the stock as the capital overhang has been structurally addressed, the major revenue from AI has already been contracted and only a fraction of its 4.5GW power portfolio is monetized.

With GPU deployments continuing to ramp up throughout 2026, the revenue from AI is expected to turn the corner, reducing the gap between the two metrics and requiring the valuation multiple to adjust accordingly.

Catalysts I Care About: The Four Tests That Move The Stock

The first one is the ability to reach a $500 million annualized AI run rate as some kind of psychologically important milestone because it means AI revenues aren’t a segment anymore, they’re a stabilizer. Prince George will reach this as an annualized contracted amount. I’m not going to start celebrating until I see it reported as AI revenues but it’s the right thing to look at.

Second, it’s another hyperscaler deal with a power portfolio that’s just too large to ignore. Now IREN has over 4.5GW of secured power. The message from management has always been that the $3.4 billion ARR goal by year-end '26 only accounts for about 10% of this secured power. Therefore, it’s fair to say that the overwhelming majority of this megawatt potential remains unallocated. The moment another large counterparty signs on to one of these deals, Sweetwater or Oklahoma, revenue visibility increases significantly without adding any new grid risk.

Third, it's the acceleration of the GPU commissioning process because the monetization gap really is the entire story. Sure, construction may be complete, civil work may be underway, but revenue doesn’t start until GPUs are delivered, energized, and in service. The market will care less about construction updates and more about billable megawatts.

Fourth, it’s Bitcoin stabilization. While I don’t need to see Bitcoin go up significantly to believe in the thesis of AI I do need to see it not go down significantly as we’re ramping up our business. Mining’s share of our business continues to decline as a percentage of our revenue mix. However, it’s an important contributor to our quarterly dynamics.

The Capital De-Risk Is Real But It Creates Expectations

If IREN had announced this Microsoft deal without a credible funding model in place, it would be a news headline with a hangover. What they actually did here was secure $3.6 billion in delayed draw GPU financing at attractive rates. Additionally, they secured significant customer pre-payments, which cover about 95% of the GPU-related CapEx required to execute on this initial deployment.

That’s not just financial engineering. That’s validation that lenders are underwriting contracted AI cash flows. But then I realize solving capital for one contract does not remove the risks. The risks are still there. The risks have merely changed. The investors will now assume that this contract structure will be replicable. All future large contracts will be compared to this one.

The Microsoft deal is a five-year contract with staged deployments in 2026, amounting to approximately $9.7 billion. That's a strong endorsement for a platform. It also puts a time constraint on meeting that deployment schedule, because if commissioning is delayed, depreciation and interest will build up while AI revenues don't, and that will hurt a lot more than a TAM argument will help.

Valuation: Credibility & Execution Speed

I don’t approach IREN's valuation like a typical neocloud because I believe IREN is still a transition play from a mining-focused revenue model to a contracted AI infrastructure play. I approach IREN's valuation as credibility times execution speed.

If management hits their $3.4 billion AI ARR ambition by the end of 2026, suddenly we have a different business, a different valuation discussion, because we have a scaled infrastructure play with Microsoft's average annual revenue contribution, and then British Columbia and US expansion adds on top.

What makes this potentially interesting is that, because GPU CapEx is largely matched with cash flows and amortizes with contract length, we don't have that AI CapEx cliff concern. It's still a credibility-dependent equity, however, because if commissioning speeds don't meet expectations, that multiple will compress very quickly because CapEx will still be very high.

IREN looks inexpensive relative to its AI infrastructure upside. The forward EV/Sales is around 13.9x and EV/EBITDA near 28.8x sit below or in line with peers despite contracted multi-billion ARR and underutilized 4.5GW power capacity, leaving meaningful operating leverage as AI revenue scales.

Risks: What Would Actually Break My Thesis

The only risk that can truly crash IREN is the cash flow air pocketcaused by the company’s commissioning, whereby GPUs and data centers are largely built out but revenue recognition is delayed sufficiently to cause the market to price in the funding gap.

The way it will work mechanically is that depreciation will start when assets are put in service, interest will accrue as draws occur, and operating expenses will increase with headcount and power availability. If GPU shipments slip, energization of data centers is delayed, or customer acceptance is delayed, the ARR will remain purely theoretical, while the AI revenue will remain too small to support the fixed cost structure. In that time frame, the debate will no longer be about ARR targets, it will be about dilution. Even if the contracts are still valid, the stock can move violently because the stock is the shock absorber.

My rough estimates of the probability of different outcomes. A mild version of this risk, whereby one to two quarters of slippage occur, is 30-40%. A more severe version, which causes equity to be issued, is lower, 10-15% but that is the one that can cause the stock to drop by half.

Final Take

I approach IREN investment as a catalyst-driven execution thesis, not a distant optionality thesis on infrastructure spend. I will remain long if commissioning delays tighten and ARR under contract continues to convert into reported AI revenue over 2026.
